title: Baked Salmon with Spring Pea Puree: A Quick and Tender Weeknight Dinner
promptIMG: Professional food photography prompt: "baked salmon fillet with golden crust, served on a plate with vibrant green spring pea puree, natural light, sharp focus on textures of flaky fish and creamy puree, rustic kitchen setting, subtle steam rising, balanced composition, realistic food styling, no artificial enhancements --quality 2 --ar 4:3 --stylize 300"
description: A straightforward recipe for baked salmon featuring a tender, flaky texture complemented by a fresh spring pea puree that delivers creaminess without dairy. This dish balances lightness with satisfying flavors, ideal for a weekday meal that feels both nourishing and elegant.
ingredients: 2 salmon fillets, skin-on, about 150g (5 oz) each
1 tbsp (15ml) olive oil, plus extra for drizzling
Freshly ground black pepper, to taste
1 cup (150g) fresh or frozen spring peas
Fresh mint leaves, a small handful, roughly chopped
Optional: pinch of chili flakes for subtle heat
introduction: I often turn to baked salmon when I want a meal that’s both quick and nourishing, especially on busy weeknights. This particular recipe stands out because it combines the delicate flakiness of perfectly cooked salmon with a vibrant spring pea puree that feels creamy yet contains no dairy. The puree’s bright green color and fresh aroma bring a seasonal touch, reminiscent of early spring dinners when fresh peas first appear at the market. The contrast between the warm, slightly crisp salmon skin and the smooth, cool puree creates a satisfying textural balance that keeps this dish feeling light but fulfilling. It’s a simple preparation that I’ve refined over several iterations to ensure the salmon stays tender without drying out, and the puree maintains a lively, fresh flavor without heaviness. This recipe suits anyone looking for a healthy dinner that doesn’t sacrifice flavor or requires complicated steps.

why_this_recipe_works.section: Why This Baked Salmon with Spring Pea Puree Works
why_this_recipe_works.content:
The success of this dish hinges on managing heat and moisture to achieve tender salmon and a bright, creamy puree without dairy. Baking the salmon skin-on at a moderate temperature allows the skin to crisp gently while the flesh cooks evenly through, preserving moisture and preventing dryness. The moderate heat provides enough time for the connective tissue in the fish to gently break down, resulting in a flaky texture that still holds together.

The spring pea puree is cooked just enough to soften the peas without losing their vibrant green color or fresh, sweet flavor. Pureeing with olive oil and a bit of lemon juice adds natural creaminess and brightness, creating a smooth texture without relying on heavy cream or butter. Adding fresh mint enhances the herbal notes, lending complexity without overpowering the dish.

Timing is key: the salmon’s short bake time and the quick preparation of the puree ensure both components are fresh and vibrant when served. The balance of textures—the slight crispness of the skin, the moist flakiness of the fish, and the silky smooth puree—makes this dish satisfying while maintaining lightness, ideal for a weekday meal.

ingredient_notes.salmon fillets: Choosing skin-on salmon is important because the skin protects the flesh during baking, helping retain moisture and adding a pleasing crisp texture when cooked properly. Freshness matters; fresher salmon has a clean smell and firm flesh that flakes gently when cooked. Thicker fillets around 1-1.5 inches ensure even cooking without drying out.
ingredient_notes.spring peas: Fresh spring peas provide natural sweetness and a bright green color that defines the puree. Frozen peas work well too but should be thawed and drained to prevent excess water diluting the puree. Overcooking peas can dull their color and flavor, so a brief blanch or steam is sufficient.
ingredient_notes.olive oil: Using good quality extra virgin olive oil in the puree adds richness and a fruity aroma that complements the peas and salmon. The oil emulsifies with the lemon juice and water to create a creamy texture without dairy. For baking, a neutral olive oil or light oil helps crisp the skin without smoking.

instructions.instruction: Preheat your oven to 200°C (400°F). Line a baking tray with parchment paper or lightly oil it to prevent sticking. Pat the salmon fillets dry with paper towels to remove surface moisture—this step is crucial for achieving a crisp skin.
instructions.doneness_cue: Salmon fillets feel dry to the touch on the surface, no visible moisture

instructions.instruction: Lightly brush both sides of the salmon fillets with olive oil, then season generously with salt and freshly ground black pepper. Place the fillets skin-side down on the prepared baking tray, ensuring they do not touch.
instructions.doneness_cue: Even coating of oil and seasoning, fillets arranged neatly with skin flat against tray

instructions.instruction: Bake the salmon in the preheated oven for 10-12 minutes. Cooking time depends on the thickness of the fillets; the salmon is done when the flesh turns opaque and flakes easily with a fork but still retains moisture. Avoid overcooking, which dries the fish.
instructions.doneness_cue: Edges of salmon turn light pink to opaque color, flesh flakes gently when prodded with a fork

instructions.instruction: While the salmon bakes, prepare the spring pea puree. Bring a small pot of salted water to a boil and blanch the peas for about 2 minutes until bright green and just tender. Immediately transfer peas to an ice bath to stop cooking and preserve color.
instructions.doneness_cue: Peas are vibrant green and tender but still firm to bite

instructions.instruction: Drain peas thoroughly and place them in a blender or food processor. Add the finely chopped shallot, minced garlic, fresh mint leaves, lemon juice, olive oil, and water or vegetable broth. Blend until smooth and creamy, stopping to scrape down the sides as needed. Adjust texture with additional water if necessary.
instructions.doneness_cue: Puree is smooth, bright green, and has a silky consistency without lumps

instructions.instruction: Taste the puree and season with salt, black pepper, and optional chili flakes to your preference. Keep the puree chilled or at room temperature while the salmon finishes baking.
instructions.doneness_cue: Balanced seasoning with fresh, bright flavor and mild heat if chili is added

instructions.instruction: Once the salmon is cooked, remove from oven and let it rest for 1-2 minutes. This brief rest allows juices to redistribute. Serve each fillet atop a generous spoonful of the spring pea puree. Drizzle with a little more olive oil if desired.
instructions.doneness_cue: Salmon is warm with a slightly crisp skin, puree is cool and creamy with fresh aroma

pro_tips.tip: Pat salmon completely dry before seasoning and baking.
pro_tips.reason: Surface moisture prevents the skin from crisping properly and can cause steaming instead of roasting. Dry skin ensures direct contact with heat, producing a desirable texture.

pro_tips.tip: Use a moderate oven temperature around 200°C (400°F) for even cooking.
pro_tips.reason: Higher temperatures can dry out the salmon quickly; moderate heat allows the flesh to cook gently while crisping the skin without burning.

pro_tips.tip: Blanch peas briefly and shock in ice water immediately.
pro_tips.reason: This stops the cooking process rapidly, preserving the bright green color and fresh, slightly sweet flavor essential to a vibrant puree.

pro_tips.tip: Add lemon juice and olive oil to the pea puree after blending.
pro_tips.reason: These ingredients emulsify with the peas to create a creamy texture without dairy, while lemon adds brightness that lifts the flavor.

pro_tips.tip: Rest salmon briefly after baking before serving.
pro_tips.reason: Resting allows the juices to redistribute within the fish, keeping it moist and tender when cut.

pro_tips.tip: Use fresh mint sparingly in the puree.
pro_tips.reason: Mint adds a refreshing herbal note that complements the peas without overpowering the delicate salmon flavor.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.mistake: Skipping drying the salmon skin before baking.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Moisture on the skin causes steaming in the oven rather than crisping.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Pat the skin dry thoroughly with paper towels before applying oil and seasoning.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.mistake: Overcooking the salmon leading to dry, tough flesh.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Salmon cooks quickly; leaving it in the oven too long causes moisture loss.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Check for doneness at 10 minutes and remove when flesh flakes easily but still looks moist.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.mistake: Overcooking peas when preparing the puree.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Peas boiled too long lose bright color and fresh flavor.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Blanch peas for only 2 minutes then immediately cool in ice water to retain vibrancy.

common_mistakes_to_avoid.mistake: Adding too much liquid to the puree, resulting in a watery texture.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.why_it_happens: Excess water or broth dilutes the puree and reduces creaminess.
common_mistakes_to_avoid.fix: Add liquid gradually while blending, stopping when the desired smooth consistency is reached.

variations.name: Dairy-Free Herb Variation
variations.description: Replace mint with fresh basil or tarragon in the pea puree for a different herbal profile while keeping it dairy-free and light.
variations.technical_note: Basil adds a sweet, peppery note, while tarragon introduces a subtle anise flavor. Both herbs blend smoothly and maintain the puree’s fresh character.

variations.name: Lemon-Garlic Butter Salmon
variations.description: Finish the baked salmon with a spoonful of melted lemon-garlic butter instead of olive oil for a richer flavor.
variations.technical_note: Butter adds fat and richness that contrasts with the pea puree. Use clarified butter to avoid burning during broiling if finishing under high heat.

variations.name: Spicy Spring Pea Puree
variations.description: Add a small fresh green chili or a pinch of cayenne pepper to the pea puree to introduce a gentle heat that complements the salmon.
variations.technical_note: Spicy elements should be added sparingly and balanced with lemon and olive oil to prevent overpowering the delicate flavors.

variations.name: Roasted Asparagus Side
variations.description: Serve the salmon and pea puree alongside oven-roasted asparagus tossed with olive oil, salt, and pepper for added texture and seasonal freshness.
variations.technical_note: Roasting asparagus at the same temperature as the salmon allows for efficient cooking, and the slightly charred tips add a contrasting flavor.

variations.name: Pea Puree with Avocado
variations.description: Incorporate half a ripe avocado into the pea puree for extra creaminess and a subtle buttery taste without dairy.
variations.technical_note: Avocado blends smoothly with peas and olive oil, enhancing texture and adding healthy fats. Use ripe, soft avocado for best results.

nutrition.note: Values are estimates and may vary based on salmon size, olive oil quantity, and pea freshness. No added cream or butter keeps fat moderate.

storage_and_reheating.room_temperature: Do not leave cooked salmon or pea puree at room temperature for more than 2 hours to avoid bacterial growth.
storage_and_reheating.refrigerator: Store salmon and pea puree separately in airtight containers for up to 2 days. The salmon may lose some crispness but remains tender.
storage_and_reheating.freezer: Freezing is not recommended for best texture, but if necessary, wrap salmon tightly and freeze for up to 1 month. Thaw overnight in the refrigerator.
storage_and_reheating.reheating: Reheat salmon gently in a low oven (150°C / 300°F) for 8-10 minutes to avoid drying. Warm pea puree briefly in a microwave or on the stovetop, stirring to prevent separation.
storage_and_reheating.make_ahead_strategy: Prepare the pea puree a few hours ahead and refrigerate. Bake the salmon just before serving to maintain optimal texture and flavor contrast.

faq.question: Can I make baked salmon with spring pea puree ahead of time?
faq.answer: You can prepare the pea puree up to 24 hours in advance and store it refrigerated. For best texture, bake the salmon just before serving.

faq.question: Why is my salmon dry after baking?
faq.answer: Dry salmon often results from overcooking or baking at too high a temperature. Use a moderate oven and check for doneness early to keep the flesh moist.

faq.question: Can I use frozen salmon fillets for this recipe?
faq.answer: Yes, but thaw completely and pat dry before baking to ensure even cooking and crispy skin.

faq.question: Can I substitute frozen peas for fresh in the puree?
faq.answer: Frozen peas can be used; just blanch and cool them as with fresh peas to preserve color and flavor.

faq.question: How long does baked salmon last in the fridge?
faq.answer: Cooked salmon should be consumed within 2 days when stored in an airtight container in the refrigerator.

faq.question: Can I reheat the pea puree?
faq.answer: Yes, warm it gently on the stovetop or in short bursts in the microwave, stirring frequently to maintain smoothness.

conclusion: This baked salmon with spring pea puree combines simple techniques and fresh ingredients to create a meal that feels thoughtfully prepared without complexity. I welcome your comments and experiences with this recipe, especially any personal touches or adaptations you discover.
